title: Heat waves mess with your brain. Scientists are trying to figure out why.
gist: Extreme heat waves demonstrably impair cognition, worsen mental health outcomes, and disproportionately harm children and people with psychiatric conditions. Researchers are still mapping the biological mechanisms — altered neurotransmitters, disrupted brain networks, reduced oxygen delivery — that make heat dangerous to the brain. As climate change multiplies heat-wave frequency, the public-health and economic costs of heat-driven cognitive and psychiatric harm are becoming a serious systemic risk.

insights.claim: Firefighters exposed to just 15 minutes of intense heat showed significantly reduced ability to focus and control attention, with cognitive function not returning to normal for at least 20 minutes after cooling, suggesting even short heat exposure creates measurable productivity and safety deficits.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Short-duration heat exposure sufficient to impair worker cognition has direct implications for labor productivity, workplace safety liabilities, and insurance costs in heat-exposed industries.
insights.quote: firefighters found it harder to focus and control their attention immediately after heat exposure

insights.claim: The duration of cognitive impairment from multi-day heat waves remains scientifically unmeasured, representing a significant gap in risk modeling for heat-related productivity losses.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Investors in climate adaptation, health tech, and labor-intensive sectors should note that the full economic drag of prolonged heat waves on worker output is likely underestimated.
insights.quote: Thompson doesn't know what the effects of living through a days-long heat wave might be—or how long they'll last.

themes.theme: Mental Health Crisis Amplified by Heat

insights.claim: Hospital admissions for people with mental-health conditions rise 9.7% during heat waves, according to a 2023 Oxford University review, indicating a quantifiable surge in healthcare system demand during climate-driven heat events.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Health systems and insurers face measurable capacity and cost pressures from heat waves, creating demand for mental-health infrastructure investment and climate-resilient hospital design.
insights.quote: during heat waves, there was a 9.7% increase in the rate of hospital admissions for people with such conditions.

insights.claim: People with schizophrenia were three times more likely to die during Canada's 2021 record-breaking heat wave, highlighting psychiatric patients as a uniquely high-risk cohort during extreme heat events.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Public health systems and long-term care operators face disproportionate mortality risk for their most vulnerable populations during increasingly frequent heat events.
insights.quote: People with schizophrenia were found to have been three times more likely to die during the record-breaking heat wave that affected Canada in 2021

insights.claim: Mental-health outcomes worsen as temperatures exceed regional norms, with the correlation between heat intensity and psychiatric harm intensifying at the hottest seasonal periods, suggesting non-linear risk escalation as climate baselines shift.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Climate change will not merely increase average heat stress but will disproportionately raise tail-risk mental-health events, pressuring insurers and health systems in warmer regions.
insights.quote: There seems to be a correlation where the hotter it gets, especially during the hottest times of the year, the worse the mental-health outcomes

insights.claim: Every 1°C rise in average monthly temperature is associated with a 2.97% increase in the US suicide rate among people aged 15–24, more than double the rate increase seen in adults over 24, identifying young people as a disproportionately heat-sensitive demographic for suicide risk.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Youth mental-health platforms, insurers covering adolescent behavioral health, and public-health agencies face escalating demand driven directly by temperature increases.
insights.quote: a 2.97% increase in the suicide rate among people in the US aged 15 to 24 for every 1 °C increase in average monthly temperature

insights.claim: Babies exposed to extreme heat or cold showed altered white matter in the brain by ages 9–12, suggesting temperature extremes during early life may cause lasting structural changes to brain development with unknown long-term functional consequences.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Pediatric neurodevelopment is an emerging vector of climate risk; companies in early childhood health and brain development diagnostics may see growing research and commercial interest.
insights.quote: Babies who were exposed to either extreme heat or cold appeared to have altered white matter by the time they were nine to 12 years old

insights.claim: Children born in 2020 are predicted to experience approximately seven times as many heat waves as their grandparents, dramatically compounding lifetime exposure to heat-related brain and mental-health risks.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Generational compounding of heat exposure creates a long-run structural demand driver for mental-health services, cognitive support technology, and climate adaptation infrastructure.
insights.quote: Children born in 2020 are predicted to experience around seven times the number of heat waves their grandparents did

themes.theme: Biological Mechanisms Linking Heat to Brain Dysfunction

insights.claim: Animal studies show that elevated temperatures increase serotonin levels in the brain, suggesting heat may biochemically disrupt mood-regulating neurotransmitter systems, potentially explaining observed increases in irritability and violence during heat waves.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: If heat-driven serotonin dysregulation is confirmed in humans, pharmaceutical and mental-health therapeutics targeting serotonergic pathways could become climate-adaptation health products.
insights.quote: The levels of neurotransmitters like serotonin, for example, seem to increase when rats and mice are exposed to high temperatures

insights.claim: Excessive heat may simultaneously disrupt brain network communication and impair oxygen delivery to brain cells, indicating multiple parallel biological pathways by which heat degrades cognitive and psychiatric function.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Multi-mechanism brain vulnerability to heat suggests no single pharmaceutical or intervention will fully mitigate cognitive harm, raising the value of systemic cooling and urban heat mitigation infrastructure.
insights.quote: There are so many biological reasons why brains may be negatively affected by heat

insights.claim: Multiple studies show rising temperatures correlate with increased human irritability and violence, though the causal mechanisms remain poorly understood because studies rely primarily on associations rather than controlled experiments.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Heat-driven upticks in violence and social instability represent an underappreciated systemic risk for businesses in affected regions, including insurance, retail, real estate, and security sectors.
insights.quote: as temperatures rise, people seem to get more irritable and more violent. Most of these studies are based on associations, though.

insights.claim: Real-time cognitive testing of people during active heat waves has not been done at scale because of logistical difficulty, leaving a major evidence gap in understanding the full economic and health impact of heat waves on populations.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: There is an unaddressed market for remote cognitive monitoring technology deployable during climate events, representing an opportunity for digital health and wearable companies.
insights.quote: My guess [is] that no one's done it because it's just so difficult to do

insights.claim: Indirect lifestyle effects of heat waves — disrupted sleep, reduced exercise, and social isolation — compound the direct neurological harm, making it difficult to isolate specific brain mechanisms and complicating public-health intervention design.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Holistic heat-health interventions addressing sleep quality, social connection, and physical activity alongside cooling may deliver greater mental-health ROI than single-focus approaches.
insights.quote: Sleep, socializing, and exercise are all really important for our mental health.

themes.theme: Climate Change as a Systemic Mental Health Risk Driver

insights.claim: Western Europe's June 2026 heat wave pushed the UK to its highest-ever recorded June temperature of 36.1°C, marking continued acceleration of climate extremes in regions not historically adapted to extreme heat.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Recurring record-setting heat in temperate economies accelerates the need for climate-adaptation investment across healthcare, infrastructure, and real estate in Europe.
insights.quote: the UK recorded its highest ever June temperature at 36.1 °C (about 97 °F)

insights.claim: England's average June temperature between 1991–2020 was 19°C, making the 2026 heat wave's 36.1°C peak nearly double historical norms, illustrating the scale of departure from baseline that climate change is producing.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: The gap between historical climate baselines and current extremes means existing health and infrastructure systems are increasingly mis-sized for the actual risk environment.
insights.quote: June temperatures peaked at an average 19 °C (66 °F) in England between 1991 and 2020

insights.claim: European heat waves are expected to cause thousands of deaths with downstream consequences for agriculture, infrastructure, and health systems, signaling that heat is now a multi-sector systemic risk beyond a purely environmental concern.
insights.speaker: Jessica Hamzelou
insights.so_what: Investors need to price heat-wave frequency into multi-sector risk models — agriculture supply chains, infrastructure operators, and healthcare systems all face material increasing costs.
insights.quote: Across Europe, the heat wave is likely to cause thousands of deaths. There will be other awful consequences for agriculture, infrastructure, and the health system.
